summary: I'm originally from Kentucky, specifically Bullitt County near Shepherdsville. Growing up in Kentucky was great because we have four equal length seasons, which means every few months you experience a new kind of weather. That variety really sparked my interest in meteorology. Later in my career, I returned to Kentucky to work as the state climatologist and director of the Kentucky Climate Center, helping to develop the Kentucky Mesonet.

I grew up in Bullitt County, Kentucky, near Shepherdsville. It’s where my roots are, and it’s where I first developed my love for weather. Being from Bullitt County gave me a strong connection to the state, which later influenced my decision to come back and work here as the state climatologist and director of the Kentucky Climate Center.

Shepherdsville, Kentucky, is the town near where I grew up in Bullitt County. It was my home before I went off to college at St. Louis University. Growing up near Shepherdsville, I experienced the full range of Kentucky’s weather, which really fueled my passion for meteorology.

I chose to attend Saint Louis University for my undergraduate studies because it was the closest university with a meteorology program near where I lived in Kentucky. I started there at age eighteen and completed my undergraduate degree before moving on to graduate school. It was a key step in my journey to becoming a meteorologist.

I actually visited Western Kentucky University because I was offered a full scholarship there, but they didn’t have a meteorology program at the time, so I didn’t enroll. Now, I’m back at WKU as a professor, the state climatologist, and director of the Kentucky Climate Center. It’s rewarding to be involved with the university and help connect students to meteorology and climate research.

I went to the University of Oklahoma for my master's and PhD in meteorology, graduating in 2000. During my time there, I worked on weather radar and numerical modeling, and I got involved with the Oklahoma Mesonet when my original advisor had a heart attack. After graduating, I stayed on at OU working with the Center for Analysis and Prediction of Storms, focusing on improving numerical weather models.
cues: Center for Analysis and Prediction of Storms

entity: Center for Analysis and Prediction of Storms
summary: After earning my PhD at the University of Oklahoma, I worked at the Center for Analysis and Prediction of Storms, or CAPS. My role involved numerical modeling and using observational data to improve weather prediction models. CAPS was a great place to apply my research skills and deepen my understanding of storm prediction.

During my graduate studies at the University of Oklahoma, I became involved with the Oklahoma Mesonet, which had just started installing its network. The Mesonet provided funding and opportunities for me to work with weather observations, which shaped my career path into mesoscale meteorology. This experience was foundational and later helped me lead Mesonet projects in other states.

In 2014, I moved to New York to help start a statewide Mesonet there after the state received FEMA funding. I led the development of a network with 126 stations plus a Micronet in New York City. This role allowed me to expand my experience with observational networks and connect with many state climatologists, including Stu Foster from Kentucky.

The Federal Emergency Management Agency, or FEMA, provided funding to New York in 2014 to start their own Mesonet program. This funding was crucial for establishing the network of 126 stations that I helped develop. FEMA’s support highlights the importance of observational networks for emergency management and weather preparedness.

I got to know Stu Foster while working with state climatologists during my time in New York and through my Kentucky connections. Stu is also part of this oral history collection, and knowing him helped me when I saw the position open back in Kentucky. Our professional relationship and shared Kentucky roots played a role in my decision to return and take on the state climatologist role.

I have a personal connection to Letcher County, Kentucky, because my uncle was a forest ranger there. He worked at a forest museum that had a small weather station, part of the cooperative observer network. Visiting him and helping record weather data at that station was my first real introduction to meteorology and weather observation.

I serve as the director of the Kentucky Climate Center, which houses both the state climate office and the Kentucky Mesonet. The center is primarily a service organization for the state, providing climate data and research support. We also involve students in research and outreach, helping to build the next generation of meteorologists in Kentucky.

The Kentucky Mesonet is a network of weather stations across the state that I oversee as director. Currently, we have 82 stations covering about 74 of Kentucky’s 120 counties, with plans to expand to one station per county. Our staff includes scientists and technicians who maintain the network from our base at the Innovation Center in Bowling Green. The Mesonet provides critical data for research and public service.

The Innovation Center in Bowling Green, Kentucky, also known as the Center for Research and Development, is where the Kentucky Mesonet staff are located. It’s housed in an old mall off Nashville Road, a couple of miles south of WKU’s campus. This facility supports our work maintaining the Mesonet and conducting climate research for the state.

Bowling Green, Kentucky, is where Western Kentucky University is located and near where the Kentucky Mesonet’s Innovation Center is based. I’m a professor at WKU and direct the Kentucky Climate Center from here. Bowling Green serves as the hub for much of my work connecting students, research, and climate services in the state.

One of our Kentucky Mesonet field technicians lives in Georgetown, Kentucky. Having staff located remotely like this helps us cover the eastern half of the state more efficiently, saving time and resources. It’s a practical arrangement that supports the maintenance and operation of our weather stations across Kentucky.

We recently hired a new Kentucky Mesonet employee who will live in Corbin, Kentucky. This helps us cover the eastern part of the state better and is a cost-effective way to manage our network. Having staff in places like Corbin ensures timely maintenance and support for our stations in that region.

Oklahoma was a formative place in my career where I completed graduate school and began working with the Mesonet and CAPS. The state’s early investment in Mesonet technology gave me valuable experience in weather observations and numerical modeling. My time in Oklahoma laid the groundwork for my later leadership roles in Mesonet programs in New York and Kentucky.
cues: Center for Analysis and Prediction of Storms
